title: Cat Jaw Locked Open: Emergency Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat's jaw is locked open, it is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn about TMJ luxation, causes, and why you need immediate care.
content_html:
What is a Locked Jaw in Cats?

When a cat is unable to close its mouth, it is frequently due to a condition called Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) luxation, where the jaw bone has slipped out of its socket. Other potential causes include a mandibular fracture, a foreign object (like a bone or stick) lodged in the back of the throat, or severe dental disease. This condition is physically distressing and prevents your cat from eating, drinking, or grooming properly.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. This is a High-Urgency emergency. A cat with a locked jaw is likely experiencing significant pain and is at risk for dehydration and respiratory issues if they are unable to swallow their own saliva. You should contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately. Do not attempt to force the jaw shut at home, as this can cause further structural damage or result in an accidental bite due to your cat's pain response.

How a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the clinic, taking a quick, clear photo of your cat's face can be very helpful. A photo allows the veterinary team to see the alignment of the jaw before muscle spasms or swelling worsen. This helps them prepare the necessary diagnostic tools, such as X-rays or sedation, before you even walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can a cat's jaw lock open from yawning?
faqs.answer: Yes, in cats with underlying joint laxity or previous injuries, a wide yawn can occasionally trigger a TMJ luxation where the joint slips out of its normal track.

faqs.question: Will my cat need surgery for a locked jaw?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. While some dislocations can be manually reduced by a vet under sedation, fractures or chronic joint issues may require surgical intervention.

faqs.question: How can I transport my cat safely to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure, well-ventilated carrier lined with soft towels. Avoid touching the face or mouth area to prevent causing more pain or stress during transit.
